_comment: Hand-labeled ground truth for `applicable_industries` on catalog rows.
The structural check in tagging.py only sees tags that are ABSENT on an
industry-specific category. It cannot see a tag that is simply WRONG — a
wage rule tagged `healthcare`, say, which then hides from every other tenant.
That is what these labels measure (micro-averaged precision/recall).
Match key is (state, city, requirement_key) against the NORMALIZED key —
so a minimum-wage row stored under rate_type `general` is labeled as
`state_minimum_wage` at state level or `local_minimum_wage` at city level.
`expected_industries: []` means the requirement is universal and MUST carry
no tag — an untagged universal row is correct, and tagging it would wrongly
hide it from companies in other industries.
Seeded from rows observed in dev on 2026-07-09. Extend opportunistically:
when adjudicating a tagging finding, add the row here with its correct tags.
Labels are curated by a human; a wrong label silently mis-scores the suite.

labels.note: Universal wage floor — applies to every employer in the city.
AB 1228 covers only chains with 60+ national establishments.
SB 525 applies only to covered health care facilities.
IWC Wage Orders 4/5 alternative workweek for healthcare.
Universal — Labor Code 510 applies to all non-exempt employees.
EPCRA 313 Toxic Release Inventory reporting is triggered by covered NAICS manufacturing sectors. Untagged in dev today, so it reaches every tenant.
Correctly tagged `healthcare` in dev — a true positive for precision.
Clinical prescribing rule. Untagged in dev, so it leaks to non-healthcare tenants.
NY Labor Law 167 binds health care employers only.
